question: What is the subject matter of Article 1 of the GDPR Regulation?
gold_answer: Article 1 lays down rules relating to the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data and rules relating to the free movement of personal data.
llm_response: Article 1 of the GDPR establishes its **scope** as rules for the protection of natural persons regarding personal data processing and the **free movement** of such data within the EU. Key subject matters include: (1) fundamental rights protection (e.g., privacy) and (2) data transfer freedoms. The regulation’s core subject matter is defined by **Chapter I, Article 1, Point 1** and **Point 2**.

question: What is the material scope of Article 2 of the GDPR Regulation?
gold_answer: Article 2 of the GDPR Regulation applies to the processing of personal data wholly or partly by automated means and to the processing other than by automated means of personal data which form part of a filing system or are intended to form part of a filing system. It does not apply to the processing of personal data in the course of an activity which falls outside the scope of Union law, by the Member States when carrying out activities which fall within the scope of Chapter 2 of Title V of the TEU, by a natural person in the course of a purely personal or household activity, or by competent authorities for the purposes of the prevention, investigation, detection or prosecution of criminal offences or the execution of criminal penalties, including the safeguarding against and the prevention of threats to public security.
llm_response: The material scope of **Article 2 of GDPR** excludes **processing of personal data by purely personal or household activities** (e.g., private use) and **Union institutions' processing** governed by Regulation (EC) No 45/2001. It applies to automated/filing-system processing unless explicitly excluded.

question: To which processing activities does Article 3 of the GDPR Regulation apply when personal data of data subjects who are in the Union is processed by a controller or processor not established in the Union?
gold_answer: Article 3 of the GDPR Regulation applies to the processing of personal data of data subjects who are in the Union by a controller or processor not established in the Union, where the processing activities are related to (a) the offering of goods or services, irrespective of whether a payment of the data subject is required, to such data subjects in the Union; or (b) the monitoring of their behaviour as far as their behaviour takes place within the Union.
llm_response: Article 3(2) of the GDPR applies to **offering goods/services to Union data subjects** (e.g., marketing, sales) **or monitoring their behavior within the Union**, regardless of whether the controller/processor is non-Union established. Processing activities explicitly listed include: (a) offering goods/services to Union data subjects, (b) monitoring behavior within the Union. No other processing activities are explicitly mentioned under this subparagraph.

question: What is the definition of 'processing' according to Article 4 of the GDPR Regulation?
gold_answer: 'Processing' means any operation or set of operations which is performed on personal data or on sets of personal data, whether or not by automated means, such as collection, recording, organisation, structuring, storage, adaptation or alteration, retrieval, consultation, use, disclosure by transmission, dissemination or otherwise making available, alignment or combination, restriction, erasure or destruction.
llm_response: 'Processing' under GDPR means any operation or set of operations performed on personal data or sets of personal data, whether automated or manual, including collection, recording, organisation, structuring, storage, adaptation, alteration, retrieval, consultation, use, disclosure by transmission, dissemination, alignment, combination, restriction, erasure, or destruction. Key snippet: ‘processing’ means any operation or set of operations which is performed on personal data or on sets of personal data, whether or not by automated means, such as collection, recording, organisation, structuring, storage, adaptation or alteration, retrieval, consultation, use, disclosure by transmission, dissemination or otherwise making available, alignment or combination, restriction, erasure or destruction.

question: What is the definition of 'cross-border processing' according to Article 4?
gold_answer: Either processing of personal data which takes place in the context of the activities of establishments in more than one Member State of a controller or processor in the Union where the controller or processor is established in more than one Member State; or processing of personal data which takes place in the context of the activities of a single establishment of a controller or processor in the Union but which substantially affects or is likely to substantially affect data subjects in more than one Member State.
llm_response: 'cross-border processing' under GDPR (Article 4) includes: (1) processing by establishments in multiple Member States of a controller/processor; (2) processing by a single establishment affecting data subjects in multiple Member States. Key snippet: ‘cross-border processing’ means either processing of personal data which takes place in the context of the activities of establishments in more than one Member State of a controller or processor in the Union where the controller or processor is established in more than one Member State; or processing of personal data which takes place in the context of the activities of a single establishment of a controller or processor in the Union but which substantially affects or is likely to substantially affect data subjects in more than one Member State.’

question: What are the six principles relating to processing of personal data according to Article 5?
gold_answer: The six principles relating to processing of personal data according to Article 5 are: (a) processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner in relation to the data subject (‘lawfulness, fairness and transparency’); (b) collected for specified, explicit and legitimate purposes and not further processed in a manner that is incompatible with those purposes (‘purpose limitation’); (c) adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which they are processed (‘data minimisation’); (d) accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date (‘accuracy’); (e) kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed (‘storage limitation’); and (f) processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against unauthorised or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or damage, using appropriate technical or organisational measures (‘integrity and confidentiality’).
llm_response: The six principles from **Article 5 GDPR** are:     1. **Lawfulness, fairness, transparency** (1a);     2. **Purpose limitation** (1b);     3. **Data minimisation** (1c);     4. **Accuracy** (1d);     5. **Storage limitation** (1e);     6. **Integrity and confidentiality** (1f).     Key snippet: *‘Personal data shall be: processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner... kept in a form which permits identification... processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security.’*

question: Under what circumstances is processing of personal data considered lawful according to Article 6?
gold_answer: Processing of personal data is considered lawful if at least one of the following applies: (a) the data subject has given consent to the processing of his or her personal data for one or more specific purposes; (b) processing is necessary for the performance of a contract to which the data subject is party or in order to take steps at the request of the data subject prior to entering into a contract; (c) processing is necessary for compliance with a legal obligation to which the controller is subject; (d) processing is necessary in order to protect the vital interests of the data subject or of another natural person; (e) processing is necessary for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority vested in the controller; (f) processing is necessary for the purposes of the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party, except where such interests are overridden by the interests or fundamental rights and freedoms of the data subject which require protection of personal data, in particular where the data subject is a child.
llm_response: Processing of personal data under **Article 6(1)** of GDPR is lawful if it complies with at least one of the following conditions: explicit consent (1a), necessity for contract execution (1b), compliance with legal obligations (1c), performance of official tasks (1e), vital interests protection (1d), or legitimate interests pursued by the controller (1f), except where overriding rights of data subjects are violated. Special categories require explicit consent, vital interests, public health, employment/social security, or public interest (Article 9).

grounding: chapter II, article 7, point 2
question: What should the request for consent be presented as, if the data subject's consent is given in the context of a written declaration which also concerns other matters?
gold_answer: The request for consent shall be presented in a manner which is clearly distinguishable from the other matters, in an intelligible and easily accessible form, using clear and plain language.
llm_response: The consent request **must be presented in a clearly distinguishable, intelligible, and easily accessible manner** using clear and plain language, separated from other matters in the written declaration. This ensures the data subject’s consent is valid and distinguishable from other clauses. Rule: **Chapter II, Article 7, Point 2**

grounding: chapter_ii_article_8_point_1, chapter_ii_article_8_point_2
question: Under what conditions is the processing of personal data of a child lawful in relation to the offer of information society services directly to a child according to Article 8?
gold_answer: The processing of personal data of a child shall be lawful where the child is at least 16 years old. Where the child is below the age of 16 years, such processing shall be lawful only if and to the extent that consent is given or authorised by the holder of parental responsibility over the child. Member States may provide by law for a lower age for those purposes provided that such lower age is not below 13 years.
llm_response: The processing of personal data of a child for information society services is lawful **only if the child is at least 16 years old** or if consent is given or authorized by the holder of parental responsibility when the child is below 16. The controller must verify parental consent reasonably, using available technology. **Article 8(1), point 1**

question: Under what circumstances is processing of personal data revealing racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religious or philosophical beliefs, or trade union membership allowed?
gold_answer: Processing of personal data revealing racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religious or philosophical beliefs, or trade union membership is allowed if the data subject has given explicit consent to the processing of those personal data for one or more specified purposes, except where Union or Member State law provide that the prohibition referred to in paragraph 1 may not be lifted by the data subject; processing is necessary for the purposes of carrying out the obligations and exercising specific rights of the controller or of the data subject in the field of employment and social security and social protection law in so far as it is authorised by Union or Member State law or a collective agreement pursuant to Member State law providing for appropriate safeguards for the fundamental rights and the interests of the data subject; processing is necessary to protect the vital interests of the data subject or of another natural person where the data subject is physically or legally incapable of giving consent; processing is carried out in the course of its legitimate activities with appropriate safeguards by a foundation, association or any other not-for-profit body with a political, philosophical, religious or trade union aim and on condition that the processing relates solely to the members or to former members of the body or to persons who have regular contact with it in connection with its purposes and that the personal data are not disclosed outside that body without the consent of the data subjects; processing relates to personal data which are manifestly made public by the data subject; processing is necessary for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims or whenever courts are acting in their judicial capacity; processing is necessary for reasons of substantial public interest, on the basis of Union or Member State law which shall be proportionate to the aim pursued, respect the essence of the right to data protection and provide for suitable and specific measures to safeguard the fundamental rights and the interests of the data subject; processing is necessary for reasons of public interest in the area of public health, such as protecting against serious cross-border threats to health or ensuring high standards of quality and safety of health care and of medicinal products or medical devices, on the basis of Union or Member State law which provides for suitable and specific measures to safeguard the rights and freedoms of the data subject, in particular professional secrecy; processing is necessary for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes in accordance with Article 89(1) based on Union or Member State law which shall be proportionate to the aim pursued, respect the essence of the right to data protection and provide for suitable and specific measures to safeguard the fundamental rights and the interests of the data subject.
llm_response: Processing of sensitive personal data (racial/ethnic origin, political opinions, religious beliefs, trade union membership) is prohibited **unless** one of the following valid legal bases applies: explicit consent (Rule_Article9_2_A), employment/social security obligations (Rule_Article9_2_B), vital interests protection (Rule_Article9_2_C), not-for-profit bodies with safeguards (Rule_Article9_2_D), manifestly made public by data subject (Rule_Article9_2_E), legal claims (Rule_Article9_2_F), substantial public interest (Rule_Article9_2_G), preventive/occupational medicine (Rule_Article9_2_H), public health (Rule_Article9_2_I), or archiving/research/statistical purposes (Rule_Article9_2_J).

grounding: chapter_ii_article_11
question: What are the obligations of a controller if the purposes for which they process personal data do not require identification of the data subject?
gold_answer: If the purposes for which a controller processes personal data do not or do no longer require the identification of a data subject by the controller, the controller shall not be obliged to maintain, acquire or process additional information in order to identify the data subject for the sole purpose of complying with this Regulation.
llm_response: The controller **may not** maintain, acquire, or process additional identifying information when processing personal data without requiring identification of the data subject. This is explicitly permitted by **Article II, point 1** of the provided rules.

question: What measures should the controller take to provide information to the data subject under Articles 13 and 14 and any communication under Articles 15 to 22 and 34?
gold_answer: The controller shall take appropriate measures to provide any information referred to in Articles 13 and 14 and any communication under Articles 15 to 22 and 34 relating to processing to the data subject in a concise, transparent, intelligible and easily accessible form, using clear and plain language, in particular for any information addressed specifically to a child. The information shall be provided in writing, or by other means, including, where appropriate, by electronic means. When requested by the data subject, the information may be provided orally, provided that the identity of the data subject is proven by other means.
llm_response: The controller **must** provide information under **Articles 13 and 14** by disclosing all required details at collection time (Art. 13) or upon first contact (Art. 14), including rights (Art. 14.2), purposes, legal bases, and data sources. For **Articles 15–22**, the controller must:     1. **Art. 15**: Confirm processing, provide access, and disclose data (Art. 15.1–3).     2. **Art. 16**: Rectify inaccurate data (Art. 16.1).     3. **Art. 17**: Erase data promptly (Art. 17.1) and notify recipients (Art. 17.2).     4. **Art. 18**: Restrict processing (Art. 18.1) and inform data subjects (Art. 18.3).     5. **Art. 20**: Offer data portability (Art. 20.1–2).     6. **Art. 21**: Explicitly highlight objection rights (Art. 21.4) and cease processing upon valid objection (Art. 21.1).     7. **Art. 22**: Notify automated decisions (Art. 22.1–4).     8. **Art. 34**: Communicate breaches (Art. 34.1) and justify non-communication (Art. 34.3) via public measures if disproportionate effort applies (Art. 34.3c).

question: What information must the controller provide to the data subject at the time when personal data are obtained, according to Article 13?
gold_answer: The controller must provide the data subject with the identity and contact details of the controller and, where applicable, of the controller's representative; the contact details of the data protection officer, where applicable; the purposes of the processing for which the personal data are intended as well as the legal basis for the processing; where the processing is based on point (f) of Article 6(1), the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party; the recipients or categories of recipients of the personal data, if any; where applicable, the fact that the controller intends to transfer personal data to a third country or international organisation and the existence or absence of an adequacy decision by the Commission, or in the case of transfers referred to in Article 46 or 47, or the second subparagraph of Article 49(1), reference to the appropriate or suitable safeguards and the means by which to obtain a copy of them or where they have been made available. In addition, the controller must provide the data subject with the period for which the personal data will be stored, or if that is not possible, the criteria used to determine that period; the existence of the right to request from the controller access to and rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing concerning the data subject or to object to processing as well as the right to data portability; where the processing is based on point (a) of Article 6(1) or point (a) of Article 9(2), the existence of the right to withdraw consent at any time, without affecting the lawfulness of processing based on consent before its withdrawal; the right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority; whether the provision of personal data is a statutory or contractual requirement, or a requirement necessary to enter into a contract, as well as whether the data subject is obliged to provide the personal data and of the possible consequences of failure to provide such data; the existence of automated decision-making, including profiling, referred to in Article 22(1) and (4) and, at least in those cases, meaningful information about the logic involved, as well as the significance and the envisaged consequences of such processing for the data subject.
llm_response: The controller **must** provide the data subject, at the time personal data are obtained, with:     1) Identity and contact details of the controller (and representative if applicable) (Point 1a);     2) Purposes of processing and legal basis (Point 1c);     3) Recipients of data or categories (Point 1e);     4) Contact of DPO (if applicable) (Point 1b);     5) Period of storage or criteria for determining it (Point 2a);     6) Rights (access, rectification, erasure, restriction, objection, data portability, complaint) (Point 2b);     7) Obligations regarding data provision (statutory/contractual necessity) (Point 2e);     8) Legitimate interests (if applicable) (Point 1d).     Key snippet: *‘Where personal data relating to a data subject are collected from the data subject, the controller shall, at the time when personal data are obtained, provide the data subject with all of the following information:’* (Chapter III, Article 13, Point 1).

gold_answer: The controller must provide the data subject with the period for which the personal data will be stored or the criteria used to determine that period, the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party if the processing is based on point (f) of Article 6(1), the right to request access to and rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing concerning the data subject and to object to processing as well as the right to data portability, the existence of the right to withdraw consent at any time if processing is based on point (a) of Article 6(1) or point (a) of Article 9(2), the right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority, from which source the personal data originate, and if applicable, whether it came from publicly accessible sources, the existence of automated decision-making, including profiling, referred to in Article 22(1) and (4) and, at least in those cases, meaningful information about the logic involved, as well as the significance and the envisaged consequences of such processing for the data subject.

question: What information must the controller provide to the data subject when they request access to their personal data under Article 15?
gold_answer: The controller must provide confirmation as to whether or not personal data concerning the data subject are being processed, and, where that is the case, access to the personal data and the following information: (a) the purposes of the processing; (b) the categories of personal data concerned; (c) the recipients or categories of recipient to whom the personal data have been or will be disclosed, in particular recipients in third countries or international organisations; (d) where possible, the envisaged period for which the personal data will be stored, or, if not possible, the criteria used to determine that period; (e) the existence of the right to request from the controller rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing of personal data concerning the data subject or to object to such processing; (f) the right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority; (g) where the personal data are not collected from the data subject, any available information as to their source; (h) the existence of automated decision-making, including profiling, referred to in Article 22(1) and (4) and, at least in those cases, meaningful information about the logic involved, as well as the significance and the envisaged consequences of such processing for the data subject.
llm_response: The controller **must provide confirmation of processing**, access to personal data, and the following mandatory information: categories of data (1b), purposes (1a), recipients (1c), storage period (1d), source (1g), and right to lodge a complaint (1f). Additional details include safeguards for transfers (2) and automated processing (1h).

grounding: chapter_iii_article_16_point_text_1
question: What is the right granted to the data subject in Article 16 of the GDPR?
gold_answer: The right to obtain from the controller without undue delay the rectification of inaccurate personal data concerning him or her. Taking into account the purposes of the processing, the data subject shall have the right to have incomplete personal data completed, including by means of providing a supplementary statement.
llm_response: The data subject has the right to request the controller rectify inaccurate or incomplete personal data under Article 16(1) GDPR, ensuring accuracy and completeness. Controller must act without undue delay.

question: What are the grounds for a data subject to request erasure of their personal data according to Article 17?
gold_answer: The grounds for a data subject to request erasure of their personal data according to Article 17 are: (a) the personal data are no longer necessary in relation to the purposes for which they were collected or otherwise processed; (b) the data subject withdraws consent on which the processing is based according to point (a) of Article 6(1), or point (a) of Article 9(2), and where there is no other legal ground for the processing; (c) the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21(1) and there are no overriding legitimate grounds for the processing, or the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21(2); (d) the personal data have been unlawfully processed; (e) the personal data have to be erased for compliance with a legal obligation in Union or Member State law to which the controller is subject; (f) the personal data have been collected in relation to the offer of information society services referred to in Article 8(1).
llm_response: The grounds for erasure under Article 17(1) include: (1a) when data is no longer necessary for original purposes; (1b) withdrawal of consent without other legal basis; (1c) objection under Article 21(1)/(2); (1d) unlawful processing; (1e) legal obligation; (1f) collection for info society services. The controller must erase without undue delay when these apply.

question: What are the circumstances under which a data subject can request restriction of processing according to Article 18?
gold_answer: A data subject can request restriction of processing if (a) the accuracy of the personal data is contested, (b) the processing is unlawful and the data subject opposes erasure, (c) the controller no longer needs the data but the data subject requires it for legal claims, or (d) the data subject has objected to processing pending verification of legitimate grounds.
llm_response: A data subject **may restrict processing** when:     1) Accuracy is contested (point 1a);     2) Processing is unlawful and data subject opposes erasure (point 1b);     3) Controller no longer needs data but it’s required for legal claims (point 1c).     The restriction applies only to storage, processing with consent, legal claims, or protection of rights (point 2).

grounding: chapter_iii_article_19_point_text_1
question: What is the notification obligation of the controller regarding rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing according to Article 19 of the GDPR?
gold_answer: The controller shall communicate any rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing carried out in accordance with Article 16, Article 17(1) and Article 18 to each recipient to whom the personal data have been disclosed, unless this proves impossible or involves disproportionate effort. The controller shall inform the data subject about those recipients if the data subject requests it.
llm_response: The controller **must notify recipients** of rectification, erasure, or restriction of processing unless impossible or disproportionate effort. The controller also **shall inform the data subject** about recipients upon request. Rule applies to disclosures made under Articles 16, 17(1), or 18.

grounding: chapter III, article 20, point 1, chapter III, article 20, point 1a, chapter III, article 20, point 1b, chapter III, article 20, point 3
question: What is the right granted to the data subject in Article 20 of the GDPR?
gold_answer: The data subject shall have the right to receive the personal data concerning him or her, which he or she has provided to a controller, in a structured, commonly used and machine-readable format and have the right to transmit those data to another controller without hindrance from the controller to which the personal data have been provided.
llm_response: The data subject has the right to receive personal data in a structured, machine-readable format and transmit it directly to another controller, where processing is based on consent or contract, and automated means are used. Exceptions apply if processing is for public interest or official authority (§3).

grounding: chapter_iii_article_21_point_1, chapter_iii_article_21_point_2, chapter_iii_article_21_point_3, chapter_iii_article_21_point_5
question: What is the right granted to the data subject in Article 21?
gold_answer: The data subject shall have the right to object, on grounds relating to his or her particular situation, at any time to processing of personal data concerning him or her which is based on point (e) or (f) of Article 6(1), including profiling based on those provisions.
llm_response: The data subject has the right to object to processing based on Art6(1)(e) or (f), requiring the controller to cease processing unless compelling legitimate grounds override the data subject’s interests (Art21(1)). For direct marketing, the data subject may object, ceasing processing for such purposes (Art21(2) & (3)). Automated objection via technical specifications is allowed (Art21(5)).

grounding: chapter III, article 22, point 1, chapter III, article 22, point 2a, chapter III, article 22, point 2b, chapter III, article 22, point 2c
question: What is the right of the data subject under Article 22?
gold_answer: The data subject shall have the right not to be subject to a decision based solely on automated processing, including profiling, which produces legal effects concerning him or her or similarly significantly affects him or her.
llm_response: The data subject under **Article 22 of GDPR** has the **right to object to automated decisions** producing legal effects or significantly affecting them, unless justified by Union/Member State law or explicit consent. The right is not absolute: exceptions apply if the decision is authorized by law or based on consent (points 1 and 2a/2c).

question: Under what circumstances can Union or Member State law restrict the scope of the obligations and rights provided for in Articles 12 to 22 and Article 34, as well as Article 5?
gold_answer: Union or Member State law may restrict the scope of the obligations and rights provided for in Articles 12 to 22 and Article 34, as well as Article 5, when such a restriction respects the essence of the fundamental rights and freedoms and is a necessary and proportionate measure in a democratic society to safeguard national security, defence, public security, the prevention, investigation, detection or prosecution of criminal offences or the execution of criminal penalties, including the safeguarding against and the prevention of threats to public security, other important objectives of general public interest of the Union or of a Member State, in particular an important economic or financial interest of the Union or of a Member State, including monetary, budgetary and taxation a matters, public health and social security, the protection of judicial independence and judicial proceedings, the prevention, investigation, detection and prosecution of breaches of ethics for regulated professions, a monitoring, inspection or regulatory function connected, even occasionally, to the exercise of official authority in the cases referred to in points (a) to (e) and (g), the protection of the data subject or the rights and freedoms of others, or the enforcement of civil law claims.
llm_response: Union or Member State law **may** restrict the scope of obligations/rights in Articles 12–22 and 5 under **legal obligations** (e.g., compliance with Union/Member State law, professional secrecy, or public interest tasks) as explicitly permitted in **Article 17(1e,3b)** and **Article 18(2)**. Restrictions must comply with Union law (e.g., GDPR) and safeguard rights (e.g., judicial remedies).

question: What measures should the controller implement to ensure and demonstrate that processing is performed in accordance with the GDPR Regulation?
gold_answer: The controller shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures, reviewed and updated where necessary, taking into account the nature, scope, context and purposes of processing as well as the risks of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons. Where proportionate in relation to processing activities, the measures shall include the implementation of appropriate data protection policies.
llm_response: The controller **must implement** appropriate **technical and organisational measures** (Art 24(1)) and **data protection policies** (Art 24(2)) to ensure and demonstrate compliance with GDPR. These measures shall be **reviewed and updated** where necessary, covering risks, safeguards, and security measures (Art 35(7d), Art 32). Records of processing activities (Art 30) and regular testing/evaluation of measures (Art 32(1d)) are also required to demonstrate compliance continuously.

question: What are the two types of measures that the controller must implement according to Article 25?
gold_answer: The controller must implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to implement data-protection principles, such as data minimisation, and to ensure that only necessary personal data are processed.
llm_response: The controller must implement **data protection by design** (Article 25(1)) and **data protection by default** (Article 25(2)) as the two mandatory measures. Key snippet: *‘Controller shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures at the time of design and processing to integrate data protection principles and safeguards’ (Rule_Article25_1) and ‘Controller shall implement by default that only personal data necessary for each specific purpose are processed’ (Rule_Article25_2).*

gold gounding: chapter IV, article 26, point 1, chapter IV, article 26, point 2, chapter IV, article 26, point 3
question: What are the responsibilities of joint controllers under Article 26 of the GDPR Regulation?
gold_answer: Joint controllers must determine their respective responsibilities for compliance with the obligations under this Regulation, in particular as regards the exercising of the rights of the data subject and their respective duties to provide the information referred to in Articles 13 and 14, by means of an arrangement between them unless, and in so far as, the respective responsibilities of the controllers are determined by Union or Member State law to which the controllers are subject. The arrangement may designate a contact point for data subjects. The arrangement shall duly reflect the respective roles and relationships of the joint controllers vis-à-vis the data subjects. The essence of the arrangement shall be made available to the data subject. Irrespective of the terms of the arrangement referred to in paragraph 1, the data subject may exercise his or her rights under this Regulation in respect of and against each of the controllers.
llm_response: Under GDPR, **joint controllers** must establish a transparent arrangement (Art. 26(1)) defining their respective responsibilities, including compliance with data subject rights (e.g., access, rectification) and duties to provide info under Art. 13/14. Each controller remains individually liable for its obligations, and data subjects can exercise rights against any joint controller (Art. 26(3)). The arrangement must reflect roles and be disclosed to data subjects (Art. 26(2)).

question: When is a controller or processor not established in the Union exempt from designating a representative in the Union under Article 27?
gold_answer: The obligation laid down in paragraph 1 of this Article shall not apply to: (a) processing which is occasional, does not include, on a large scale, processing of special categories of data as referred to in Article 9(1) or processing of personal data relating to criminal convictions and offences referred to in Article 10, and is unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons, taking into account the nature, context, scope and purposes of the processing; or (b) a public authority or body.
llm_response: A controller or processor **not established in the Union** may **not designate a representative** under **Article 27(1)** only if **Article 3(2)** explicitly exempts them from this obligation. The exemption applies when processing is limited to **non-Union entities** and does not involve offering goods/services or monitoring behavior of EU data subjects. No further exceptions are explicitly stated in the provided chunks.

question: What are the obligations of a processor under a contract or other legal act with the controller, according to Article 28?
gold_answer: The obligations of a processor under a contract or other legal act with the controller, according to Article 28, include processing personal data only on documented instructions from the controller, ensuring confidentiality of persons authorized to process the data, taking all measures required pursuant to Article 32, respecting the conditions for engaging another processor, assisting the controller by appropriate technical and organizational measures, assisting the controller in ensuring compliance with the obligations pursuant to Articles 32 to 36, deleting or returning all the personal data to the controller after the end of the provision of services relating to processing, making available to the controller all information necessary to demonstrate compliance with the obligations laid down in this Article and allow for and contribute to audits, including inspections, conducted by the controller or another auditor mandated by the controller, and immediately informing the controller if, in its opinion, an instruction infringes this Regulation or other Union or Member State data protection provisions.
llm_response: Under **Article 28(3)**, the processor’s obligations include: (a) processing data only on documented controller instructions; (b) ensuring confidentiality of authorized personnel; (c) assisting the controller in compliance with **Articles 32–36** (e.g., risk assessments, security measures); (d) providing access to compliance evidence for audits; (e) deleting/returning data upon service termination unless legally required. **Point 3a** explicitly requires adherence to controller instructions, while **Point 3f** mandates support for compliance with data protection laws. No explicit rule covers *all* responsibilities in the question’s phrasing, but these are core obligations under the contract.

grounding: chapter IV, article 29, point text_1, chapter IV, article 32, point 4
question: Under what circumstances can the processor or any person acting under the authority of the controller or processor process personal data?
gold_answer: The processor and any person acting under the authority of the controller or of the processor, who has access to personal data, shall not process those data except on instructions from the controller, unless required to do so by Union or Member State law.
llm_response: The **processor** or any person acting under its authority may process personal data **only on documented instructions from the controller** (unless required by Union/Member State law), as per **Chapter IV, Article 29, Point text_1**. The processor must inform the controller of any legal requirement before processing unless prohibited by public interest. No independent processing is permitted without explicit instructions or legal obligation.

question: What information should be included in the record of processing activities maintained by a controller according to Article 30?
gold_answer: The record of processing activities maintained by a controller should contain the name and contact details of the controller and, where applicable, the joint controller, the controller's representative and the data protection officer; the purposes of the processing; a description of the categories of data subjects and of the categories of personal data; the categories of recipients to whom the personal data have been or will be disclosed including recipients in third countries or international organisations; where applicable, transfers of personal data to a third country or an international organisation, including the identification of that third country or international organisation and, in the case of transfers referred to in the second subparagraph of Article 49(1), the documentation of suitable safeguards; where possible, the envisaged time limits for erasure of the different categories of data; where possible, a general description of the technical and organisational security measures referred to in Article 32(1).
llm_response: The record of processing activities maintained by a controller under Article 30(GDPR) **must** include:     1) Name/contact details of the controller/data protection officer (1a);     2) Purposes of processing (1b);     3) Categories of data subjects/personal data (1c);     4) Recipients of data (1d);     5) Transfers to third countries with safeguards (1e);     6) Security measures (1g);     7) Time limits for erasure (1f).     Key snippet: *‘...all of the following information: the name and contact details... the purposes of the processing... the categories of data subjects... the categories of recipients... transfers of personal data... a general description of the technical and organisational security measures... envisaged time limits for erasure.’*

question: Who is required to cooperate with the supervisory authority upon request in the performance of its tasks according to Article 31 of the GDPR?
gold_answer: The controller and the processor and, where applicable, their representatives.
llm_response: According to **Article 31(1) GDPR**, the **controller, processor, and their representatives** must cooperate with the supervisory authority upon its request to perform its tasks. The rule applies universally to these entities.

question: What are the four technical and organisational measures that the controller and processor should implement to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk?
gold_answer: The four technical and organisational measures that the controller and processor should implement to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk are: (a) the pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data; (b) the ability to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of processing systems and services; (c) the ability to restore the availability and access to personal data in a timely manner in the event of a physical or technical incident; (d) a process for regularly testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational measures for ensuring the security of the processing.
llm_response: The **four** technical and organisational measures explicitly referenced in **Article 32(1)** of the provided context are:     1) **Risk assessment** (Rule_32_2_Riskassessment);     2) **Use of codes/certification for compliance** (Rule_32_3_Usecodeorcertificationforcompliance);     3) **Limiting personnel processing** (Rule_32_4_Limitpersonnelprocessing);     4) **Regular testing, assessment, and evaluation** (Point_1d: *‘process for regularly testing, assessing and evaluating technical and organisational measures’*).      These measures are **mandatory** for both controllers and processors under **Chapter IV, Article 32(1)** to ensure a security level proportional to the risk.

grounding: chapter IV, article 33, point 1, chapter IV, article 34, point 1
question: What is the time limit for a controller to notify the supervisory authority of a personal data breach?
gold_answer: The controller shall without undue delay and, where feasible, not later than 72 hours after having become aware of it, notify the personal data breach to the supervisory authority competent in accordance with Article 55, unless the personal data breach is unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons.
llm_response: The controller must notify the supervisory authority of a personal data breach **within 72 hours** of becoming aware of it, unless the breach is unlikely to result in a risk to rights and freedoms. If notification is delayed, it must include reasons for the delay. Failure to comply may incur administrative fines up to 10 000 000 EUR or 2% of turnover.

grounding: chapter IV, article 34, point 3a, chapter IV, article 34, point 3b, chapter IV, article 34, point 3c
gold gounding: chapter IV, article 34, point 3a, chapter IV, article 34, point 3b, chapter IV, article 34, point 3c
question: When is a controller not required to communicate a personal data breach to the data subject according to Article 34?
gold_answer: The controller is not required to communicate a personal data breach to the data subject if any of the following conditions are met: (a) the controller has implemented appropriate technical and organisational protection measures, and those measures were applied to the personal data affected by the personal data breach, in particular those that render the personal data unintelligible to any person who is not authorised to access it, such as encryption; (b) the controller has taken subsequent measures which ensure that the high risk to the rights and freedoms of data subjects referred to in paragraph 1 is no longer likely to materialise; (c) it would involve disproportionate effort. In such a case, there shall instead be a public communication or similar measure whereby the data subjects are informed in an equally effective manner.
llm_response: A controller **shall not** communicate a personal data breach to the data subject if any of the conditions in **Article 34(3)** are met, such as: (a) encryption measures rendering data unintelligible to unauthorized parties, or (b) subsequent measures eliminating the high-risk scenario. Key snippet: *‘the controller has implemented appropriate technical and organisational protection measures...’ (Point 3a).

grounding: chapter_iv_article_35_point_1, chapter_iv_article_35_point_3a, chapter_iv_article_35_point_3b, chapter_iv_article_35_point_3c, chapter_iv_article_35_point_10
gold gounding: chapter IV, article 35, point 1, chapter IV, article 35, point 3a, chapter IV, article 35, point 3b, chapter IV, article 35, point 3c
question: When is a data protection impact assessment required according to Article 35?
gold_answer: A data protection impact assessment is required when a type of processing in particular using new technologies, and taking into account the nature, scope, context and purposes of the processing, is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons. It is also required in specific cases such as systematic and extensive evaluation of personal aspects relating to natural persons which is based on automated processing, including profiling, and on which decisions are based that produce legal effects concerning the natural person or similarly significantly affect the natural person; processing on a large scale of special categories of data referred to in Article 9(1), or of personal data relating to criminal convictions and offences referred to in Article 10; or a systematic monitoring of a publicly accessible area on a large scale.
llm_response: A data protection impact assessment **shall be required** when processing involves high risks (e.g., new technologies, large-scale processing of special categories of data, automated decisions affecting individuals, or systematic monitoring of public areas). Key snippet: *‘Where a type of processing... is likely to result in a high risk... the controller shall carry out an assessment’* (Article 35(1)).

grounding: chapter_iv_article_36_point_1, chapter_iv_article_36_point_5
question: When is a controller required to consult the supervisory authority prior to processing?
gold_answer: The controller shall consult the supervisory authority prior to processing where a data protection impact assessment under Article 35 indicates that the processing would result in a high risk in the absence of measures taken by the controller to mitigate the risk.
llm_response: A controller **must** consult the supervisory authority **prior to processing** when a data protection impact assessment (DPIA) under Article 36(1) indicates a high risk, unless Member State law requires prior authorisation for public-interest tasks (e.g., social protection, public health). The consultation is mandatory for high-risk processing unless exempted by law.

question: Under what circumstances must a controller or processor designate a data protection officer?
gold_answer: A controller or processor must designate a data protection officer in any case where: a) the processing is carried out by a public authority or body, except for courts acting in their judicial capacity; b) the core activities of the controller or the processor consist of processing operations which, by virtue of their nature, their scope and/or their purposes, require regular and systematic monitoring of data subjects on a large scale; or c) the core activities of the controller or the processor consist of processing on a large scale of special categories of data pursuant to Article 9 and personal data relating to criminal convictions and offences referred to in Article 10.
llm_response: A **controller or processor must designate a Data Protection Officer (DPO) when required by Article 37(1), specifically if their core activities involve large-scale monitoring or data profiling, or processing on a large scale. Additionally, they **shall** designate a DPO if Union or Member State law mandates it (Rule_37_General_Obligation).

grounding: chapter IV, article 39, point 1a, chapter IV, article 39, point 1b, chapter IV, article 39, point 1c, chapter IV, article 39, point 2
gold gounding: chapter IV, article 39, point 1a, chapter IV, article 39, point 1b, chapter IV, article 39, point 1c, chapter IV, article 39, point 1d, chapter IV, article 39, point 1e
question: What are the tasks of the data protection officer according to Article 39 of the GDPR?
gold_answer: The data protection officer shall have at least the following tasks: to inform and advise the controller or the processor and the employees who carry out processing of their obligations pursuant to this Regulation and to other Union or Member State data protection provisions; to monitor compliance with this Regulation, with other Union or Member State data protection provisions and with the policies of the controller or processor in relation to the protection of personal data, including the assignment of responsibilities, awareness-raising and training of staff involved in processing operations, and the related audits; to provide advice where requested as regards the data protection impact assessment and monitor its performance pursuant to Article 35; to cooperate with the supervisory authority; to act as the contact point for the supervisory authority on issues relating to processing, including the prior consultation referred to in Article 36, and to consult, where appropriate, with regard to any other matter.
llm_response: The Data Protection Officer (DPO) under GDPR (Article 39) must monitor compliance with data protection laws, advise on data protection impact assessments, inform and train staff on obligations, and assess risks associated with processing operations. Key tasks include: (1a) informing/advising controllers/processors on obligations; (1b) monitoring compliance and auditing policies; (1c) providing input on data protection impact assessments. **Key snippet**: *‘to monitor compliance...; to provide advice...; to inform and advise...’*

grounding: chapter IV, article 38, point 1, chapter IV, article 38, point 3, chapter IV, article 38, point 2, chapter IV, article 38, point 4, chapter IV, article 38, point 5, chapter IV, article 38, point 6
gold gounding: chapter IV, article 38, point 1, chapter IV, article 38, point 2, chapter IV, article 38, point 3, chapter IV, article 38, point 5, chapter IV, article 38, point 6
question: What is the role of the data protection officer according to Article 38 of the GDPR?
gold_answer: The data protection officer is responsible for being involved in all issues related to the protection of personal data, performing tasks referred to in Article 39, being supported by the controller and processor with necessary resources and access to personal data, reporting directly to the highest management level, being bound by secrecy or confidentiality, and being able to fulfill other tasks and duties as long as they do not result in a conflict of interests.
llm_response: The data protection officer (**DPO**) under GDPR (**Article 38**) must be involved in all data protection-related matters (**Point 1**), report directly to the highest management (**Point 3**), and ensure secrecy (**Point 5**). The DPO may fulfill additional tasks without conflict (**Point 6**), while controllers/processors must support their work (**Point 2**) and protect them from dismissal/punishment for fulfilling duties. Data subjects can contact the DPO regarding their rights (**Point 4**).

question: What is the purpose of codes of conduct under Article 40 of the GDPR Regulation?
gold_answer: The purpose of codes of conduct under Article 40 of the GDPR Regulation is to contribute to the proper application of the Regulation, taking account of the specific features of the various processing sectors and the specific needs of micro, small and medium-sized enterprises.
llm_response: Codes of conduct under **Article 40 GDPR** serve to **specify the application of the Regulation** (e.g., fair processing, security measures, dispute resolution) and **enable mandatory monitoring** by the body under Article 41(1) for adherence by controllers/processors. They may also apply to non-regulated entities for transfers to third countries under safeguards (Article 40(3)).

question: What are the criteria that a body must meet to be accredited to monitor compliance with a code of conduct under Article 41?
gold_answer: The body must demonstrate its independence and expertise in relation to the subject-matter of the code to the satisfaction of the competent supervisory authority, establish procedures to assess the eligibility of controllers and processors concerned to apply the code, monitor their compliance with its provisions and periodically review its operation, establish procedures and structures to handle complaints about infringements of the code or the manner in which the code has been, or is being, implemented by a controller or processor, and demonstrate to the satisfaction of the competent supervisory authority that its tasks and duties do not result in a conflict of interests.
llm_response: A body must demonstrate **independence and expertise** in the code’s subject-matter (point 2a), establish **assessment procedures** for controllers/processors (point 2b), and have **complaint-handling mechanisms** transparent to data subjects (point 2c). These criteria are explicitly set out in **Chapter IV, Article 41, Point 2** of the regulation.

question: What is the purpose of data protection certification mechanisms and seals according to Article 42?
gold_answer: The purpose of data protection certification mechanisms and seals is to demonstrate compliance with GDPR regulations of processing operations by controllers and processors, and to establish appropriate safeguards for personal data transfers to third countries or international organizations.
llm_response: The purpose of data protection certification mechanisms and seals under **Article 42** of GDPR is to **demonstrate compliance** with the Regulation by controllers/processors, including addressing micro, small, and medium-sized enterprises' needs. They are voluntary, transparent, and issued by certification bodies or supervisory authorities based on approved criteria, ensuring safeguards for data subjects (e.g., via the **European Data Protection Seal**). They do not exempt controllers/processors from their obligations under GDPR.

gold gounding: chapter IV, article 43, point 2a, chapter IV, article 43, point 2b, chapter IV, article 43, point 2c, chapter IV, article 43, point 2d, chapter IV, article 43, point 2e
question: What are the requirements for certification bodies to be accredited in accordance with paragraph 1 of Article 43?
gold_answer: Certification bodies referred to in paragraph 1 shall be accredited in accordance with that paragraph only where they have demonstrated their independence and expertise in relation to the subject-matter of the certification to the satisfaction of the competent supervisory authority; undertaken to respect the criteria referred to in Article 42(5) and approved by the supervisory authority which is competent pursuant to Article 55 or 56 or by the Board pursuant to Article 63; established procedures for the issuing, periodic review and withdrawal of data protection certification, seals and marks; established procedures and structures to handle complaints about infringements of the certification or the manner in which the certification has been, or is being, implemented by the controller or processor, and to make those procedures and structures transparent to data subjects and the public; and demonstrated, to the satisfaction of the competent supervisory authority, that their tasks and duties do not result in a conflict of interests.
llm_response: Certification bodies must be accredited by the **national accreditation body** under **Regulation (EC) No 765/2008** (EN-ISO/IEC 17065:2012) and approved by the supervisory authority (Article 43(1b)). They must demonstrate independence, expertise, and compliance with criteria set by the supervisory authority (Article 43(2a-b)).

question: Under what conditions can a transfer of personal data to a third country or international organization take place according to Article 44 of the GDPR?
gold_answer: A transfer of personal data to a third country or international organization can take place only if the conditions laid down in Chapter V of the GDPR are complied with by the controller and processor, including for onward transfers of personal data from the third country or an international organization to another third country or to another international organization.
llm_response: A transfer of personal data to a third country or international organization under GDPR **must** comply with **Article 44**, requiring **appropriate safeguards** (e.g., binding corporate rules, standard contractual clauses) and **enforceable data subject rights + legal remedies**. The controller/processor must ensure the **level of protection** in the EU is not undermined. No transfer is permitted without these safeguards.

question: What are the requirements for certification bodies to be accredited in accordance with paragraph 1 of Article 43?
gold_answer: Certification bodies referred to in paragraph 1 shall be accredited in accordance with that paragraph only where they have demonstrated their independence and expertise in relation to the subject-matter of the certification to the satisfaction of the competent supervisory authority; undertaken to respect the criteria referred to in Article 42(5) and approved by the supervisory authority which is competent pursuant to Article 55 or 56 or by the Board pursuant to Article 63; established procedures for the issuing, periodic review and withdrawal of data protection certification, seals and marks; established procedures and structures to handle complaints about infringements of the certification or the manner in which the certification has been, or is being, implemented by the controller or processor, and to make those procedures and structures transparent to data subjects and the public; and demonstrated, to the satisfaction of the competent supervisory authority, that their tasks and duties do not result in a conflict of interests.
llm_response: Certification bodies must be accredited by the **national accreditation body** under Regulation (EC) No 765/2008, aligned with **EN-ISO/IEC 17065:2012** and additional supervisory authority requirements (point 1b). gold gounding: chapter V, article 45, point 2a, chapter V, article 45, point 2b, chapter V, article 45, point 2c
question: What are the elements that the Commission takes into account when assessing the adequacy of the level of protection in a third country or international organization, according to Article 45?
gold_answer: The Commission shall take account of the following elements: a) the rule of law, respect for human rights and fundamental freedoms, relevant legislation, both general and sectoral, including concerning public security, defence, national security and criminal law and the access of public authorities to personal data, as well as the implementation of such legislation, data protection rules, professional rules and security measures, including rules for the onward transfer of personal data to another third country or international organisation which are complied with in that country or international organisation, case-law, as well as effective and enforceable data subject rights and effective administrative and judicial redress for the data subjects whose personal data are being transferred; b) the existence and effective functioning of one or more independent supervisory authorities in the third country or to which an international organisation is subject, with responsibility for ensuring and enforcing compliance with the data protection rules, including adequate enforcement powers, for assisting and advising the data subjects in exercising their rights and for cooperation with the supervisory authorities of the Member States; and c) the international commitments the third country or international organisation concerned has entered into, or other obligations arising from legally binding conventions or instruments as well as from its participation in multilateral or regional systems, in particular in relation to the protection of personal data.
llm_response: The Commission assesses adequacy by considering: (1) rule of law, human rights, and enforcement of data protection laws (point 2a); (2) independent supervisory authorities with enforcement powers (point 2b); (3) international commitments and legal obligations (point 2c). Key snippet: *‘rule of law, respect for human rights, relevant legislation, independent supervisory authorities, international commitments’*

grounding: chapter V, article 46, point 1, chapter V, article 49, point 1
question: Under what conditions can a controller or processor transfer personal data to a third country or international organization?
gold_answer: A controller or processor may transfer personal data to a third country or an international organisation only if the controller or processor has provided appropriate safeguards, and on condition that enforceable data subject rights and effective legal remedies for data subjects are available.
llm_response: A controller or processor may transfer personal data to a third country or international organization **only if appropriate safeguards** (e.g., contractual clauses, adequacy decisions, or binding corporate rules) are in place **and enforceable data subject rights/legal remedies** exist. Key rules: **Article 46(1)** and **Article 49(1)** require these conditions.

grounding: chapter V, article 48, point text_1
question: Under what circumstances can a judgment of a court or tribunal and any decision of an administrative authority of a third country requiring a controller or processor to transfer or disclose personal data be recognised or enforceable?
gold_answer: Any judgment of a court or tribunal and any decision of an administrative authority of a third country requiring a controller or processor to transfer or disclose personal data may only be recognised or enforceable in any manner if based on an international agreement, such as a mutual legal assistance treaty, in force between the requesting third country and the Union or a Member State, without prejudice to other grounds for transfer pursuant to this Chapter.
llm_response: Under GDPR, a third-country court or administrative decision requiring data transfer/disclosure **shall only be recognised/enforceable if based on an international agreement (e.g., mutual legal assistance treaty) between the third country and the EU/Member State**, or if the decision is **consistent with Article 46 safeguards** (e.g., adequacy decision, binding corporate rules, or contractual clauses with enforceable data subject rights). No recognition/enforceability is granted without these conditions.

question: What are the requirements that binding corporate rules must fulfill in order to be approved by the competent supervisory authority?
gold_answer: Binding corporate rules must be legally binding and apply to every member concerned of the group of undertakings, expressly confer enforceable rights on data subjects with regard to the processing of their personal data, and fulfill the requirements laid down in paragraph 2 of Article 47.
llm_response: Binding corporate rules must specify at least: (1) application of GDPR principles (e.g., purpose limitation, data minimisation), (2) rights of data subjects (including redress/complaint mechanisms), (3) compliance verification mechanisms (audits, corrective actions), (4) liability acceptance for breaches by group members, (5) reporting/recording changes to supervisory authorities, and (6) designated compliance roles. Approval by the competent supervisory authority requires adherence to these criteria under **Article 47(1)** and the consistency mechanism in **Article 63**.

question: Under what conditions can a transfer or set of transfers of personal data to a third country or an international organisation take place in the absence of an adequacy decision or appropriate safeguards?
gold_answer: A transfer or set of transfers of personal data to a third country or an international organisation shall take place only on one of the following conditions: (a) the data subject has explicitly consented to the proposed transfer, after having been informed of the possible risks of such transfers for the data subject due to the absence of an adequacy decision and appropriate safeguards; (b) the transfer is necessary for the performance of a contract between the data subject and the controller or the implementation of pre-contractual measures taken at the data subject's request; (c) the transfer is necessary for the conclusion or performance of a contract concluded in the interest of the data subject between the controller and another natural or legal person; (d) the transfer is necessary for important reasons of public interest; (e) the transfer is necessary for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims; (f) the transfer is necessary in order to protect the vital interests of the data subject or of other persons, where the data subject is physically or legally incapable of giving consent; (g) the transfer is made from a register which according to Union or Member State law is intended to provide information to the public and which is open to consultation either by the public in general or by any person who can demonstrate a legitimate interest, but only to the extent that the conditions laid down by Union or Member State law for consultation are fulfilled in the particular case.
llm_response: A transfer of personal data to a third country or international organisation **shall** occur only if one of the following conditions applies: explicit consent (Article 49(1a)), necessity for contract execution (Article 49(1b)), legal claims (Article 49(1e)), vital interests (Article 49(1f)), public interest (Article 49(1d)), or performance of a pre-contractual measure (Article 49(1c)). Additionally, if the transfer is not repetitive, concerns limited data subjects, and is justified by compelling legitimate interests not overriding data subjects' rights, with safeguards assessed by the controller (Article 49(1) second subparagraph).

question: What steps should the Commission and supervisory authorities take in relation to third countries and international organisations according to Article 50 of GDPR?
gold_answer: The Commission and supervisory authorities should take appropriate steps to (a) develop international cooperation mechanisms to facilitate the effective enforcement of legislation for the protection of personal data; (b) provide international mutual assistance in the enforcement of legislation for the protection of personal data, including through notification, complaint referral, investigative assistance and information exchange, subject to appropriate safeguards for the protection of personal data and other fundamental rights and freedoms; (c) engage relevant stakeholders in discussion and activities aimed at furthering international cooperation in the enforcement of legislation for the protection of personal data; (d) promote the exchange and documentation of personal data protection legislation and practice, including on jurisdic­tional conflicts with third countries.
llm_response: The Commission and supervisory authorities **must** develop cooperation mechanisms (e.g., enforce legislation for third countries and IOs), provide mutual assistance (e.g., notification, investigative support), and promote data protection standards (e.g., exchange legislation and resolve jurisdictional conflicts). Key obligations include: (1) facilitating enforcement via international cooperation (Point 1a), (2) engaging stakeholders in discussions (Point 1c), and (3) exchanging safeguards and documentation (Point 1d). Safeguards for personal data and rights apply to all assistance (Point 1b).

question: What is the purpose of the supervisory authority established in each Member State according to Article 51 of the GDPR Regulation?
gold_answer: The purpose of the supervisory authority established in each Member State is to monitor the application of the GDPR Regulation, protect the fundamental rights and freedoms of natural persons in relation to processing, and facilitate the free flow of personal data within the Union.
llm_response: The supervisory authority in each Member State under **Article 51(1) GDPR** is established to **monitor compliance with GDPR**, protect fundamental rights of natural persons regarding personal data processing, and ensure the **free flow of personal data within the Union**. Its core purpose is to enforce GDPR’s application locally.

question: What is the requirement for the member or members of each supervisory authority in performing their tasks and exercising their powers?
gold_answer: The member or members of each supervisory authority shall, in the performance of their tasks and exercise of their powers in accordance with this Regulation, remain free from external influence, whether direct or indirect, and shall neither seek nor take instructions from anybody.
llm_response: Members of supervisory authorities **must** remain free from external influence and **shall not** seek or take instructions (Article 52(2)), while their qualifications, experience, and skills are defined by Article 53(1). Their term of office is at least four years (Article 54(1d)), with prohibitions on incompatible occupations (Article 52(3)) and duty of professional secrecy (Article 54(1e)).

gold_answer: Each member shall have the qualifications, experience and skills, in particular in the area of the protection of personal data, required to perform its duties and exercise its powers.
llm_response: Each member of the supervisory authority must possess the **qualifications, experience, and skills**—specifically in the area of **protection of personal data**—to fulfill its duties and exercise powers. This requirement is explicitly stated in **Chapter VI, Article 53, Point 2**.

question: What are the six provisions that each Member State must provide by law regarding the establishment of the supervisory authority according to Article 54?
gold_answer: The six provisions that each Member State must provide by law regarding the establishment of the supervisory authority according to Article 54 are: (a) the establishment of each supervisory authority; (b) the qualifications and eligibility conditions required to be appointed as a member of each supervisory authority; (c) the rules and procedures for the appointment of the member or members of each supervisory authority; (d) the duration of the term of the member or members of each supervisory authority of no less than four years, except for the first appointment after 24 May 2016, part of which may take place for a shorter period where that is necessary to protect the independence of the supervisory authority by means of a staggered appointment procedure; (e) whether and, if so, for how many terms the member or members of each supervisory authority are eligible for reappointment; and (f) the conditions governing the obligations of the member or members and staff of each supervisory authority, prohibitions on actions, occupations and benefits incompatible therewith during and after the term of office, and rules governing the cessation of employment.
llm_response: Each Member State must by law establish the supervisory authority under Article 54(1) with provisions for:     1) **Establishment** (1a);     2) **Qualifications** (1b);     3) **Appointment rules** (1c);     4) **Term duration** (1d);     5) **Reappointment eligibility** (1e);     6) **Obligations/prohibitions** (1f).

grounding: chapter VI, article 55, point 1
question: Which Article of the GDPR Regulation specifies the competence of supervisory authorities?
gold_answer: Article 55
llm_response: The competence of supervisory authorities under GDPR is specified in **Chapter VI, Article 55, Point 1**, which states: *'Each supervisory authority shall be competent for the performance of the tasks assigned to and the exercise of the powers conferred on it in accordance with this Regulation on the territory of its own Member State.'*

question: According to Article 56, which supervisory authority shall be competent to act as lead supervisory authority for cross-border processing carried out by a controller or processor?
gold_answer: The supervisory authority of the main establishment or of the single establishment of the controller or processor shall be competent to act as lead supervisory authority for the cross-border processing carried out by that controller or processor.
llm_response: The supervisory authority of the **main establishment** or **single establishment** of the controller/processor shall act as lead supervisory authority for cross-border processing, per **Article 56(1)**. The lead authority is the sole interlocutor for such processing under **Article 56(6)**. No other authority is explicitly designated as lead unless derogations apply (e.g., local relevance in **Article 56(2)**).

question: What are the tasks of each supervisory authority according to Article 57 of the GDPR Regulation?
gold_answer: Each supervisory authority shall monitor and enforce the application of the GDPR Regulation, promote public awareness and understanding of the risks, rules, safeguards, and rights in relation to processing, advise the national parliament, the government, and other institutions and bodies on legislative and administrative measures relating to the protection of natural persons' rights and freedoms with regard to processing, promote the awareness of controllers and processors of their obligations under this Regulation, provide information to any data subject concerning the exercise of their rights under this Regulation and, if appropriate, cooperate with the supervisory authorities in other Member States to that end, handle complaints lodged by a data subject, or by a body, organization, or association in accordance with Article 80, and investigate, to the extent appropriate, the subject matter of the complaint and inform the complainant of the progress and the outcome of the investigation within a reasonable period, in particular if further investigation or coordination with another supervisory authority is necessary, cooperate with, including sharing information and provide mutual assistance to, other supervisory authorities with a view to ensuring the consistency of application and enforcement of this Regulation, conduct investigations on the application of this Regulation, including on the basis of information received from another supervisory authority or other public authority, monitor relevant developments, insofar as they have an impact on the protection of personal data, in particular the development of information and communication technologies and commercial practices, adopt standard contractual clauses referred to in Article 28(8) and in point (d) of Article 46(2), establish and maintain a list in relation to the requirement for data protection impact assessment pursuant to Article 35(4), give advice on the processing operations referred to in Article 36(2), encourage the drawing up of codes of conduct pursuant to Article 40(1) and provide an opinion and approve such codes of conduct which provide sufficient safeguards, pursuant to Article 40(5), encourage the establishment of data protection certification mechanisms and of data protection seals and marks pursuant to Article 42(1), and approve the criteria of certification pursuant to Article 42(5), where applicable, carry out a periodic review of certifications issued in accordance with Article 42(7), draft and publish the criteria for accreditation of a body for monitoring codes of conduct pursuant to Article 41 and of a certification body pursuant to Article 43, conduct the accreditation of a body for monitoring codes of conduct pursuant to Article 41 and of a certification body pursuant to Article 43, authorize contractual clauses and provisions referred to in Article 46(3), approve binding corporate rules pursuant to Article 47, contribute to the activities of the Board, keep internal records of infringements of this Regulation and of measures taken in accordance with Article 58(2), and fulfill any other tasks related to the protection of personal data.
llm_response: Each supervisory authority under GDPR (Art. 57(1)) must monitor enforcement (1a), handle complaints (1f), cooperate with other authorities (1g), conduct investigations (1h), promote public awareness (1b), advise national institutions (1c), and fulfill other data protection-related tasks (1v). Key snippet: *‘conduct investigations... cooperate... promote public awareness... fulfill any other tasks related to the protection of personal data.’*
